---
title: "Length"
subtitle: "How long are articles, and how has that changed?"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

DERIV = Path("data/derivatives")
length_dist = pd.read_csv(DERIV / "length_distribution.csv")
length_by_topic = pd.read_csv(DERIV / "length_by_topic.csv")
length_per_year = pd.read_csv(DERIV / "length_per_year.csv")
topic_totals = pd.read_csv(DERIV / "topic_totals.csv")

ACCENT = "#2ca02c"

PAGE_BIN_ORDER = ["1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6–10", "11–20", "21–50", "51–100", "101+"]
length_dist["pages_bin"] = pd.Categorical(length_dist["pages_bin"], categories=PAGE_BIN_ORDER, ordered=True)
length_by_topic["pages_bin"] = pd.Categorical(length_by_topic["pages_bin"], categories=PAGE_BIN_ORDER, ordered=True)

total_with_pages = int(length_dist["count"].sum())
excluded = 8545 - total_with_pages
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Page length is computed as `lastpage − firstpage + 1` for the
**`{python} f"{total_with_pages:,}"`** articles whose pagination is numeric.
**`{python} excluded`** articles (5.4%) — usually short front-matter with
Roman-numeral pagination such as *i, ii, iii* — are excluded from this view.
:::

## Distribution of article lengths

```{python}
#| label: fig-length-hist
fig = go.Figure(
    go.Bar(
        x=length_dist["pages_bin"].astype(str),
        y=length_dist["count"],
        marker_color=ACCENT,
        text=length_dist["count"],
        textposition="outside",
        hovertemplate="%{x} pages: %{y:,} articles<extra></extra>",
    )
)
fig.update_layout(
    height=420,
    margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=20, b=40),
    xaxis_title="Pages",
    yaxis_title="Articles",
    plot_bgcolor="white",
)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category", categoryorder="array", categoryarray=PAGE_BIN_ORDER)
fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee")
fig
```

Short pieces dominate — over half of articles run to five pages or fewer.

## Length composition by topic

For each of the top ten topics, the share of articles in each length bin.

```{python}
#| label: fig-length-by-topic
top10 = (
    length_by_topic.groupby("topic", as_index=False)["count"].sum()
    .sort_values("count", ascending=False)["topic"].tolist()
)
order_df = length_by_topic.copy()
order_df["topic"] = pd.Categorical(order_df["topic"], categories=top10, ordered=True)
order_df = order_df.sort_values(["topic", "pages_bin"])
order_df["share"] = order_df.groupby("topic", observed=True)["count"].transform(lambda s: s / s.sum())

fig = px.bar(
    order_df,
    x="topic",
    y="share",
    color="pages_bin",
    category_orders={"topic": top10, "pages_bin": PAGE_BIN_ORDER},
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.sequential.Greens[2:],
    hover_data={"count": True, "share": ":.1%"},
)
fig.update_layout(
    height=460,
    margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=10, b=40),
    xaxis_title=None,
    yaxis_title="Share of articles",
    legend_title_text="Pages",
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    barmode="stack",
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%", showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee")
fig
```

*Letter* and *Errata* skew very short; *Article* and *Astronomical Observation*
are more bimodal, with a long tail of multi-dozen-page reports.

## Median length over time

```{python}
#| label: fig-length-over-time
lpy = length_per_year.copy()
lpy["median_rolling5"] = lpy["median_pages"].rolling(window=5, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=lpy["year"],
        y=lpy["median_pages"],
        mode="markers",
        marker=dict(size=4, color=ACCENT, opacity=0.45),
        name="Yearly median",
        hovertemplate="%{x}: median %{y:.0f} pages<extra></extra>",
    )
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=lpy["year"],
        y=lpy["median_rolling5"],
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=ACCENT, width=2.5),
        name="5-year rolling mean of medians",
    )
)
fig.update_layout(
    height=420,
    margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=10, b=40),
    xaxis_title="Year",
    yaxis_title="Median pages",
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, xanchor="right", x=1),
)
fig.update_xaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee")
fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee")
fig
```

Typical article length grows substantially across the 18th and 19th centuries
— a few pages in the earliest years, more like a dozen in the late 1800s.
